title: Fitting a hinged loft hatch
subtitle: Replace a friction-fit ceiling hatch with a hinged push-up hatch and pull cord
excerpt: How to swap a simple drop-out loft hatch panel for a hinged push-up hatch with a pull cord. Covers measuring the opening, fitting the new frame, hanging the hatch on its hinges, and the cord-and-catch assembly.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: The timber frame that lines the opening in the ceiling, providing a stop for the hatch panel and a fixing surface for the hinges. Replaceable as a unit on most older hatches.
A spring-loaded catch fitted to the hatch frame that holds the closed hatch shut. Released by pushing the hatch up with a pole and lowering it gently as the catch disengages.

content.text: An older drop-out hatch lifts free of the opening and has to be carried out of the loft each time. A modern hinged push-up hatch stays attached and lowers on its
frame
with a cord-and-pole release. The swap takes half a day and the new hatch helps with insulation, since the hinged version fits a draught seal where the old loose panel could not.
Step 1, measure the existing opening

Lift the old panel out. Measure the inside of the existing timber lining left to right, then front to back. The standard opening is 562 by 762 mm but older properties have many sizes. Pick a hinged hatch unit sized to fit; standard sizes are sold at any builders' merchant, and a slightly small unit can be packed with timber laths around the rebate.
Step 2, remove the old lining

If the lining timber is in poor shape, prise it off the joists with a wrecking bar. Lay a board across the joists to stand on; do not put weight on the ceiling between joists. Patch any damaged plaster around the opening. If the old lining is sound, leave it in place and fit the new hatch unit into the existing rebate.
Step 3, fit the new frame

Slot the new frame into the opening from below. The frame should rest with its rebate against the joist faces. Square the frame to the joists with the combination square. Drill pilot holes through the frame into the joists. Drive 65 mm number 8 screws to fix the frame at four to six points.
Step 4, hang the hatch on its hinges

Most hinged hatch units come pre-hinged: the panel is already attached to the frame. If yours has separate hinges, lay the panel on the floor face down. Mark the hinge positions 100 mm in from each end. Screw the hinges to the panel. Lift the panel up to the frame and screw the other leaf of each hinge to the frame. Check the panel swings up and down freely.
Step 5, fit the push-up catch
Screw the
push-up catch

into the frame at the position marked by the supplier. The catch sits on the side opposite the hinges. Tie the supplied pull cord to the catch arm. Test the action: push the hatch up by hand, then lower it; the catch should snap shut with a click.
Step 6, fit the draught seal

Stick the supplied foam or rubber draught seal around the rebate of the frame. Press the strip firmly into the corner where the hatch meets the frame when closed. The seal stops draughts and reduces heat loss into the loft.
Step 7, fit the trim and test

Fit the supplied architrave or trim around the frame at the ceiling face. Pin with panel pins or screw with cup head screws. Test the hatch with the loft pole: push to release the catch, lower by hand, then close. The action should be smooth in both directions.
Variations

An insulated hatch panel costs more but adds the equivalent of 50 mm of loft insulation across the hatch area. Worth it on a hatch in a heated landing where the loft is unheated. A hatch above a stair landing also benefits from a soft-close hinge so the panel does not drop quickly when released.
Care

Check the catch and the draught seal once a year. Re-stick any peeling seal sections. The catch may need a drop of oil after five years; the hinges may need a drop of oil sooner if the action stiffens.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: The hatch does not stay closed
attrs.cause: The catch is misaligned with its strike, or the hatch panel is not seated in the rebate.
attrs.fix: Slacken the catch screws, adjust the catch position 2 to 3 mm at a time, retighten, and test. The strike should engage cleanly when the hatch closes.

attrs.symptom: The hatch drops quickly when the catch is released
attrs.cause: Standard hinges have no resistance once the panel is past horizontal.
attrs.fix: Replace the hinges with soft-close versions sold as a pair at any DIY merchant. Same screw centres as standard hinges.

attrs.cause: The frame is not square in the opening, or the hatch panel is twisted.
attrs.fix: Slacken the frame screws, square the frame with the combination square, retighten. If the panel is twisted, replace the panel; a twisted panel cannot be straightened reliably.
